title: Welcome Party

jokes.text: Glad they didn't mix up your tonsillectomy with a moustachectomy.
jokes.setup: Jim reading a get-well card for Stanley's tonsil surgery
jokes.punchline: Made-up medical term 'moustachectomy' playing on Stanley's facial hair
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay but the joke falls flat when it's revealed Stanley doesn't have a mustache, making it accidentally awkward rather than clever

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: "A moustachectomy? Stanley, I didn't know you were having that done."

jokes.text: Oh, because your jokes are all hilarious.
jokes.setup: Jim dismissing the card joke as 'not good'
jokes.punchline: Pam's sarcastic defense implying Jim's jokes aren't better
jokes.explanation: Standard marital banter, functional but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh right, because your jokes are SO much better.

jokes.text: Stanley doesn't have a moustache.
jokes.setup: The entire premise of the card joke about Stanley's mustache
jokes.punchline: Jim's confident assertion that Stanley lacks the key feature being joked about
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe setup - the confidence of the assertion creates immediate uncomfortable comedy as the entire card premise collapses

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Stanley, I have to say, you don't have the facial hair to pull off that look.

jokes.text: The whole card depends on this!
jokes.setup: Minor uncertainty about Stanley's facial hair
jokes.punchline: Pam's dramatic overreaction to what should be a simple observation
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of stakes for something completely trivial - classic Office absurdist panic

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Did you... did you shave your beard, Stanley?

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Yeah, I shaved this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. No, no, no, no, no. Stanley, your face. I... I can't even look at you right now. You look like a completely different person. A scary person. A very, very scary person.

jokes.text: The man's worked here for 25 years. How can none of us picture his face?
jokes.setup: Collective uncertainty about Stanley's appearance
jokes.punchline: Existential workplace horror that they don't actually notice their coworkers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational comedy that captures the absurd depersonalization of office life - both funny and genuinely dark

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen Stanley today?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I think he came in this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley Hudson is always here. He's like furniture.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, we have a Stanley?

jokes.text: 'Cause we come here to do our jobs. We don't stick our noses in other people's business.
jokes.setup: Question about why they don't know Stanley's appearance
jokes.punchline: Kevin's earnest but absurd explanation that completely misses the point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kevin logic - technically correct but completely missing the deeper absurdity of not recognizing faces

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Well, Stanley's always in his office with the door closed. And when he does come out, he's usually wearing a disguise so nobody recognizes him.

jokes.text: Neither of those looks like any person that has ever existed or been dreamt of in the history of human insanity.
jokes.setup: Being asked to choose between two terrible drawings of Stanley
jokes.punchline: Kevin's unexpectedly eloquent and devastating critique of the artwork
jokes.explanation: Spectacular character moment - Kevin suddenly becomes eloquent for maximum comedic contrast, and the phrase is genuinely poetic in its absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, they're both pretty bad. But this one... this one has a certain je ne sais quoi of incompetence that really speaks to me. It's like the artist was trying to draw Stanley, but gave up halfway through and just decided to make some kind of abstract representation of despair. And this other one? This one's worse.

jokes.text: Stanley arriving and everyone reacting to confirm he does have a mustache
jokes.setup: Entire cold open debate about whether Stanley has facial hair
jokes.punchline: Visual confirmation that resolves the debate with collective 'aha!' moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect cold open payoff - the visual reveal creates satisfying resolution while highlighting how absurd the whole debate was

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, everyone listen up. We need to settle this once and for all. Does Stanley have a beard or not?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I think he does. I've definitely seen facial hair.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No way, that's just his face. Stanley's always looked like that.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have conducted extensive visual analysis. The evidence is inconclusive.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Why are you all looking at me like this?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You've got something going on with your chin, man.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: This is the most pointless debate in the history of this office.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Stanley, stand up. Let's get a good look.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: Aaaaahhhhh!

jokes.text: Good morning, Robert, says no one, because our receptionist is in Florida.
jokes.setup: Robert arriving at work in the morning
jokes.punchline: Self-aware acknowledgment of his own unpopularity disguised as commentary on Pam's absence
jokes.explanation: Clever self-deprecating humor that works on multiple levels - Robert knows he's disliked but frames it as a staffing issue

dialogue.speaker: Robert California
dialogue.line: Where is Pam? You know, it's funny... when I walk in, nobody looks up. But when Pam doesn't show up? Suddenly everyone's concerned. I suppose that says something about me.

jokes.text: Is this a video conference you're having with... 'Drake, featuring Swizz Beatz'?
jokes.setup: Robert catching Pam watching music videos at work
jokes.punchline: Robert's formal, confused delivery of rapper names in corporate speak
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Robert's corporate formality clashing with hip-hop culture creates natural humor

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Pam, what are you doing? Are you watching music videos?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I was just taking a quick break.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: I see. Well, I'm going to need you to stop that. We have a professional environment to maintain. Now, who was that gentleman? Fifty Cent? Two Dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: It's Fifty Cent.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Right. Well, tell Fifty Cent and his associate Twenty-Five Cent that they need to clock out.

jokes.text: Nellie's elaborate snooze button story with sound effects
jokes.setup: Robert questioning why Nellie is late
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily detailed and animated recreation of hitting snooze repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Character-driven but overlong - the sound effects show Nellie's theatrical nature but the bit drags

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Why are you late?

dialogue.speaker: Nelnie
dialogue.line: Well, my alarm went off at 6 AM, and I hit snooze. Then it went off again at 6:09, and I hit snooze again. Then 6:18, snooze. 6:27, snooze. 6:36, snooze. 6:45, snooze. 6:54, snooze. 7:03, snooze. 7:12, snooze. 7:21, snooze...

jokes.text: I've never believed willpower was very important in a woman.
jokes.setup: Nellie admitting she has no willpower
jokes.punchline: Robert's casually sexist and bizarre philosophical statement
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Robert's weird pseudo-intellectual misogyny - shocking and funny because it's so casually delivered

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I have no willpower.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Willpower is like a muscle. The more you use it, the stronger it gets. Women are like water — they adapt to whatever container you put them in.

jokes.text: Are there a lot of Irish people living around here? Yes. Yes. Ugh! I hate that! No offense.
jokes.setup: Nellie complaining about moving to Scranton
jokes.punchline: Casual xenophobia immediately followed by hollow politeness
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy - the immediate 'no offense' after blatant offense creates maximum awkwardness

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I mean, Scranton? It's like being exiled to a third-world country. The people here are so... unsophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: But please, don't take that the wrong way. I'm sure you're all very nice.

jokes.text: Ay, caramba! The natives are getting restless!
jokes.setup: People getting upset at her offensive comments
jokes.punchline: Even more offensive response using cartoon Spanish and colonial language
jokes.explanation: Doubles down on the offensive humor - Nellie's complete obliviousness to her own racism is the joke

dialogue.speaker: Nelline
dialogue.line: Oh come on, it's just a little joke! Why is everyone so sensitive these days?

dialogue.speaker: Nelline
dialogue.line: Ay caramba, amigos! I was just trying to bring some spice to the office, like a conquistador bringing civilization to the New World!

jokes.text: Keep that stuff on the basketball court. Or the squash court, or the supreme court.
jokes.setup: Telling people to keep hostility elsewhere
jokes.punchline: Escalating through different types of 'courts' with increasing absurdity
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay that feels forced - the 'court' connection is tenuous and the escalation doesn't build properly

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I want everyone to keep their hostility outside of this office.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: If you have a problem with someone, take it to court.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Traffic court, small claims court, basketball court, whatever you need.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: But resolve it there, not here.

jokes.text: Why Jim?
jokes.setup: Robert assigning Jim to help move Nellie's boxes
jokes.punchline: Jim's simple, defeated question highlighting the arbitrary nature of the assignment
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words capture Jim's resignation and the absurdity of corporate arbitrary decisions

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why me?

jokes.text: We're throwing a party for someone because they're being horrible? We're taking work time to move someone's personal belongings?
jokes.setup: Robert's announcement about helping Nellie
jokes.punchline: Oscar's logical breakdown of how backwards the situation is
jokes.explanation: Classic Oscar - logical deconstruction of workplace absurdity, perfectly in character as the voice of reason

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Wait, so Robert is helping Nellie get back into the company after she basically took over and kicked everyone out? That's like... that's like helping someone break into your house after they robbed you, and then thanking them for it. The logic is completely backwards.

jokes.text: I'm still not sure why this woman is even here. Why is she here?
jokes.setup: All the fuss being made over Nellie
jokes.punchline: Stanley's blunt existential question that everyone is thinking
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stanley moment - cutting through all pretense with characteristic directness and mild hostility

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Why are we all making such a big deal about Nellie? We're all just gonna die anyway.

jokes.text: Oh, no, no, I mean that must be nice to have parents.
jokes.setup: Andy mentioning his girlfriend's parents' cabin
jokes.punchline: Erin's casual revelation of her tragic backstory
jokes.explanation: Perfectly delivered dark humor - Erin's cheerful delivery of something tragic creates uncomfortable comedy

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh, a cabin! That sounds nice. My parents had a cabin once. Well, it was more of a shack. And it burned down. With my family inside it. So that's why I'm so cheerful now.

jokes.text: Right here in the break room. Order carrot cake.
jokes.setup: Planning to throw a deliberately bad party
jokes.punchline: Angela's idea of ultimate party failure - the wrong venue and universally hated cake flavor
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Angela's specific idea of terrible hospitality shows her understanding of social norms through violation

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: If you want to throw a party that will absolutely fail, book the venue in the warehouse and serve liver cake.

jokes.text: And I'll remember you as a gentleman. Okay. That was one minute and ten seconds. Consider it nailed.
jokes.setup: What appears to be a real emotional breakup scene
jokes.punchline: Revealing it was rehearsal with Andy timing it like a performance
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - the audience believes they're witnessing real emotion before the reveal makes it comedically clinical

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I can't do this anymore. I'm sorry, but we're done.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Cut! Perfect. That was exactly 47 seconds. Good timing on the tears.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Thanks! I've been practicing in the bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It really shows. Let's go again from the top.

jokes.text: Oh, I think I'll just use this line horizontally. Oh, I had no idea what a gift this line is.
jokes.setup: Arguing about fitting furniture through doors
jokes.punchline: Dwight's weirdly specific impression of Jim playing Tetris badly
jokes.explanation: Funny character detail - Dwight has apparently watched Jim play Tetris enough to do an impression of his strategy

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You're rotating it wrong. That's not how Tetris works. You go like this—*makes exaggerated rotation motions*—'Oh no, I'm Jim Halpert, I don't understand spatial relations, I'm just gonna jam it in there sideways and hope it fits.'

jokes.text: Bunch of grown men waving wands, pulling bunnies out of body crevices.
jokes.setup: Discussion of magicians being repulsive
jokes.punchline: Nellie's disturbing and anatomically confused description of magic tricks
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor with character specificity - Nellie's particular brand of disgusted observation

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Magic is just... it's repulsive. The way they contort their bodies, the way they make things disappear into their bodies. I once saw a magician pull a rabbit out of his pants. Out of his pants! Where was it before? I don't want to know.

jokes.text: Haulin' cube! That's moving boxes. We just came up with the term to make it sound cooler.
jokes.setup: Jim saying they're 'hauling cube' on the phone
jokes.punchline: Dwight's earnest explanation of their failed attempt to make manual labor sound hip
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - his complete sincerity about obviously uncool slang makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: We were trying to make manual labor sound hip. You know, 'hauling cube.' It didn't work.

jokes.text: She needed a ride because she totaled her car.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining why Erin is with him to Jessica's family
jokes.punchline: Obvious lie told badly while the person he's lying about stands right there
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward lie scenario - functional cringe but not particularly inventive

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is Erin. She's my... administrative assistant.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I'm his girlfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: She means... intern. She's very confused about office hierarchy.

jokes.text: I wonder if king-sized sheets are called presidential-sized in england. I really should have a tweeter account.
jokes.setup: Nellie unpacking and making random observations
jokes.punchline: Two completely unrelated thoughts delivered as profound insights, plus 'tweeter' mispronunciation
jokes.explanation: Character-driven randomness - shows Nellie's scattered thinking and social media ignorance

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about this a lot. The human heart can only take so much disappointment before it simply gives up.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Also, I discovered this wonderful social media platform called 'The Tweeter.' Simply marvelous for sharing one's thoughts with the masses.

jokes.text: Here's the two of them kissing at a beach and kissing at the Eiffel Tower. I'm guessing he's some kind of close romantic friend. Like a boyfriend?
jokes.setup: Looking at romantic photos of Nellie with a man
jokes.punchline: Jim's deliberately obtuse analysis of obvious romantic evidence
jokes.explanation: Solid understatement comedy - Jim's fake confusion about obvious romantic context

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So this guy... he's just a friend?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: No, Jim. Obviously they're together.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But how can you tell? They're just standing next to each other. Could be coworkers.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: They're holding hands, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, that could mean anything. Maybe he's guiding her. She could have terrible eyesight.

jokes.text: New theory-- He's a hated Italian politician. Better theory-- This is her ex-boyfriend, and they went through some kind of painful breakup.
jokes.setup: Photo with man's face whited out
jokes.punchline: Jim's absurd first theory followed by obvious correct answer
jokes.explanation: Good comedic structure - the ridiculous first guess makes the obvious second one funnier

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So someone photoshopped this guy's face out... Maybe he's in witness protection? Or he's a vampire and doesn't show up in photos?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh wait, it's probably just Toby.

jokes.text: That's what I call my box full of photos of Henry. Why not call... Shh!
jokes.setup: Nellie explaining she calls a box 'Benjamin'
jokes.punchline: Dwight's logical question immediately shut down, highlighting the irrationality
jokes.explanation: Shows the irrationality of heartbreak through Nellie's arbitrary naming and rejection of logic

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I call my box Benjamin.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Because that's what I want to call it.

jokes.text: If it would help you to forget, I could hit you in the brain stem with this candle stick.
jokes.setup: Nellie saying the photos are painful memories
jokes.punchline: Dwight's sincere offer to cause brain damage as therapy
jokes.explanation: Peak Dwight - his literal-minded approach to problem-solving leads to casual offer of violence, delivered with complete sincerity

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: These photos are painful memories.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I can fix that. I know a very effective way to cause brain damage.

jokes.text: What kind of fool gets her heart broken by a bloody stage magician?
jokes.setup: Emotional story about her ex-boyfriend Henry
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he was a stage magician, recontextualizing the entire story
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff - recontextualizes her earlier hatred of magicians as personal trauma, perfectly structured revelation

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: Henry was the love of my life. We met in college, and he just swept me off my feet. He was so mysterious, so charming. He'd disappear for days at a time without explanation.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: I never knew where he went or what he was doing. He'd come back with these elaborate stories, but they never quite added up. I felt like I was living in a constant state of confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: One day I finally confronted him. I said, 'Henry, I need to know the truth. Where have you been? What are you hiding from me?' And he looked at me with those eyes and said...

dialogue.speaker: Nellie
dialogue.line: 'Nellie, I'm a stage magician. I've been touring the country doing shows.'

jokes.text: Call it off, Pam. Call it off, okay? It's way more complicated than you think. Cancel the magician. Trust me.
jokes.setup: Jim's sudden urgency after learning about Nellie's magician ex
jokes.punchline: Jim's panicked attempt to prevent disaster that Pam doesn't understand
jokes.explanation: Great dramatic irony - audience knows why Jim is panicking but Pam doesn't, creating tension and humor

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We need to leave. Now. Right now.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: What? Why? What's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Nellie's ex-boyfriend is a magician, Pam. A magician.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: ...Okay? So?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So?! He could make us disappear! He could make our baby disappear!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim, that's not how magic works.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You don't know that!

jokes.text: We're in far too deep. We can't change course at this point. Suck it!
jokes.setup: Pam suggesting they make the party normal instead of mean
jokes.punchline: Angela's dramatic refusal to show mercy, ending with juvenile taunt
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Angela treating party planning like military strategy, with childish ending

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: No. We are going to have the meanest party this office has ever seen. And if you don't like it, you can get out. We're going to make fun of everybody. We're going to laugh at their pain. And there's nothing you can do about it. So there.

jokes.text: Do you want a gummy penis? No, I'll just have some gummy bears. These are delicious. But... they're penises.
jokes.setup: Bachelorette party with inappropriate candy
jokes.punchline: Andy's innocent confusion about penis-shaped candy
jokes.explanation: Standard penis joke elevated slightly by Andy's obliviousness and the woman's patient explanation

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What is this candy shaped like?

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: It's a penis.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, it's anatomically incorrect.

jokes.text: Maybe we don't pop that one. No, pop Matthew. Pop it!
jokes.setup: Balloon-popping game targeting bad boyfriends while Andy relates to one
jokes.punchline: Andy trying to save the balloon representing someone like him while others demand its destruction
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Andy's personal investment in defending a cheating boyfriend while being one himself

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. That one's me. That's my balloon. I'm a bad boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Woman 1
dialogue.line: Pop it!

dialogue.speaker: Woman 2
dialogue.line: Pop it!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm trying to change!

jokes.text: Andy was Jelly Roll. Mike was Dennis The Menace. Ryan was douche bag. Hey, that's not a code name. That's just an insult.
jokes.setup: Darryl explaining warehouse code names for people
jokes.punchline: Ryan's 'code name' being just a direct insult, Jim pointing out it defeats the purpose
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - the first two names are actual code names, Ryan's breaks the pattern for maximum comedy

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: We got code names for people. Keeps things interesting in the warehouse.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Ryan's code name is 'The Temp Who Thinks He's Better Than Everyone.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Darryl, that's not a code name. That's just... what he is.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Exactly. Defeats the purpose.

jokes.text: How 'bout we go with Pam? 'Cause there's someone already here named Pam. Hey!
jokes.setup: Trying to pick a code name for Nellie
jokes.punchline: Kevin suggesting the name of someone standing right there, Pam's indignant reaction
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin logic failure - completely missing the point of code names, with perfect reaction from Pam

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: How about Pam?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Kevin, I'm standing right here.

jokes.text: They asked me to play only originals. I said, 'Have you heard my originals? They're terrible.' They said, 'Even better.'
jokes.setup: Magician performing at what he thinks is an ironic party
jokes.punchline: His realization that he was hired specifically because he's bad
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware humor - the performer understanding he's been hired to be terrible creates meta-comedy

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: So you all wanted me to perform here tonight... at this party...

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: Because you thought it would be ironic? Because a magician at a corporate party is... funny?

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: Wait. No. You didn't hire me because I'm *good* at magic, did you?

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: You hired me because I'm *bad* at magic.

jokes.text: Super-honesty time. I'm gay. I am gay, and I prefer men.
jokes.setup: Andy's increasingly desperate attempts to break up with Jessica
jokes.punchline: Choosing fake homosexuality as his escape route from the relationship
jokes.explanation: Peak Andy cowardice - using fake homosexuality to avoid difficult conversation, though somewhat problematic by today's standards

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Jessica, there's something I need to tell you. I'm... I'm gay.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: What? Andy, that doesn't make any sense. We've been together for months.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I know, and I'm sorry. I just... I've been struggling with this the whole time. I think we should break up.

dialogue.speaker: Jessica
dialogue.line: This is insane. You're making this up just to get out of this relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I'm totally serious. Very gay. So, so gay.

jokes.text: I had to imagine that I was in a steam room with John Stamos.
jokes.setup: Andy claiming he had to fake attraction to Jessica
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and random fantasy detail involving John Stamos
jokes.explanation: Randomly specific detail makes the lie funnier - John Stamos is an oddly specific choice that feels authentic to Andy's pop culture brain

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I had to fake attraction to Jessica.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I fantasized about John Stamos washing my car in the rain.

jokes.text: Every day I imagine how happy I'd be if Pam died.
jokes.setup: Robert praising Nellie while disparaging Pam
jokes.punchline: Escalating from workplace annoyance to literal death fantasy
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark escalation that perfectly captures Robert's bizarre management style and casual cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Nellie is a consummate professional. Pam, on the other hand, can barely manage to show up on time.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Nellie closes deals. Pam closes... nothing. She just sits there at that desk.

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: If Pam were any less effective, I'd have to let her go. Actually, if she were any less effective, I'd want her to go. Off a cliff. I'd push her off a cliff myself.

jokes.text: We hate Pam. We hate Pam. We hate Pam! We hate Pam!
jokes.setup: Robert's anti-Pam sentiment spreading
jokes.punchline: Group chant turning into mob mentality against someone present
jokes.explanation: Effective escalation of uncomfortable workplace dynamics into mob behavior, though somewhat one-note

dialogue.speaker: Robert
dialogue.line: Pam, you've been a real problem around here.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: She never refills the coffee!

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: And her art is terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: PAM! PAM! PAM!

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Guys, what is happening right now?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The people have spoken!

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: PAM! PAM! PAM!

jokes.text: Probably from seeing that turkey. When we drove by the farm. Ohh. Always does it.
jokes.setup: Erin saying she feels tired after their emotional scene
jokes.punchline: Andy's completely unrelated and mundane explanation for post-emotional exhaustion
jokes.explanation: Absurdist deflection from emotional moment - the mundane turkey explanation contrasts with the dramatic situation

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: I feel so tired after that emotional scene.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: That's probably because you didn't eat lunch. I had a really big breakfast, so I'm fine.

jokes.text: Big guy, huh? How's the air up there? Watch out for... birds.
jokes.setup: Magician addressing tall Dwight
jokes.punchline: Painfully bad tall person jokes ending with inexplicable bird warning
jokes.explanation: Intentionally bad comedy from the magician character - the birds line is so random it becomes funny

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: You're very tall.

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: Do you play basketball?

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: How's the weather up there?

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: You'd better watch out for low-flying birds.

jokes.text: No hablo el cardo, señor?
jokes.setup: Dwight not following card trick instructions
jokes.punchline: Magician's terrible fake Spanish about cards
jokes.explanation: Weak racist humor that's meant to show the magician's terribleness but isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You're supposed to pick a card, any card.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I know what I'm doing.

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: Okay, now put it back in the deck.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Already did.

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: That was fast. Now I'm going to find your card using ancient magic. El card-o has been selected-o. The card-o is in the deck-o. Uno, dos, tres card-os!

jokes.text: Before I was a magician, I used to work... at a rope factory. 'Cause that's not a real place.
jokes.setup: Magician starting what seems like a personal anecdote
jokes.punchline: Jim's immediate factual correction destroying the bit
jokes.explanation: Good interruption comedy - Jim's literal-minded destruction of obvious fiction is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: So I was at this restaurant, right? And the waiter comes up to me and says, 'Sir, we have a problem.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That didn't happen.

dialogue.speaker: Magician
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That didn't happen. You weren't at a restaurant.

jokes.text: Okay, scram, wizard.
jokes.setup: Magician's act completely falling apart
jokes.punchline: Dwight's blunt dismissal using 'wizard' instead of 'magician'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - the word choice 'wizard' is both dismissive and oddly formal, very in character

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That wizard is a hack.

jokes.text: This cake has vegetables in it. Like a salad bar, Robert.
jokes.setup: Kevin complaining about carrot cake
jokes.punchline: Kevin's confused analogy comparing cake to a salad bar
jokes.explanation: Classic Kevin food confusion - his analogy doesn't quite work but shows his simple food categories

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Carrot cake is not cake. It's a salad. You eat salad to be healthy. Cake is supposed to be bad for you. That's the whole point of cake.

jokes.text: Super-duper honesty time. I'm not gay.
jokes.setup: Andy's earlier fake gay confession
jokes.punchline: Immediate reversal with even more dramatic language
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure - 'super-duper' escalates the 'super-honesty' formula while immediately contradicting himself

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. I need to take that back. I'm not gay. I'm actually... extremely heterosexual. Like, aggressively heterosexual. I'm so heterosexual that other heterosexual men feel inadequate around me.

jokes.text: I can't believe you're not gay!
jokes.setup: Andy's confession reversal causing chaos
jokes.punchline: Woman's angry response about his heterosexuality
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of the mob anger, though the line itself isn't particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Bachelorette party woman
dialogue.line: Are you serious?! You mean to tell me you're straight?

jokes.text: You don't think he could have used... It couldn't have been...
jokes.setup: Learning that a magician got past security
jokes.punchline: Hank's dawning realization that the magician might have used actual magic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Hank's simple worldview leads him to consider actual magic as explanation for security failure

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: A magician? Got past security? How'd he do that?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: He just... walked right through the door?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Wait. Wait a minute.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Was it... real magic?

tmdb_overview: Robert California forces the office to throw a welcome party for Nellie, but the Party Planning Committee works on ways to sabotage it. Meanwhile, Erin tries to help Andy figure out a way to break up with Jessica.
insight: Cringe escalation carries 51 jokes through a middling episode as the office welcomes Robert California.
